texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We study high-frequency semiparametric inference for ergodic regime-switching jump diffusions whose continuous coefficients are parametric and whose regime-wise Lévy densities are unknown. The motivation is that jumps contaminate increments while their law is itself unknown, making likelihood-based inference circular in switching models. We propose a two-stage procedure. First, small increments are used in a truncated Gaussian quasi-likelihood to estimate the drift and diffusion parameters. Second, large drift-corrected residuals are sorted by regime and smoothed with a kernel, with normalization by empirical regime exposure time, to estimate the Lévy intensity densities on compact sets away from zero. We establish consistency and mixed-rate asymptotic normality for the quasi-maximum likelihood estimator, and derive L 2 ( B ) -convergence rates for the exposure-normalized residual density estimator. Simulations for switching Ornstein-Uhlenbeck models illustrate the finite-sample performance of the method.
texts.text: We study high-frequency semiparametric inference for ergodic regime-switching jump diffusions whose continuous coefficients are parametric and whose regime-wise Lévy densities are unknown. The motivation is that jumps contaminate increments while their law is itself unknown, making likelihood-based inference circular in switching models. We propose a two-stage procedure. First, small increments are used in a truncated Gaussian quasi-likelihood to estimate the drift and diffusion parameters. Second, large drift-corrected residuals are sorted by regime and smoothed with a kernel, with normalization by empirical regime exposure time, to estimate the Lévy intensity densities on compact sets away from zero. We establish consistency and mixed-rate asymptotic normality for the quasi-maximum likelihood estimator, and derive L 2 ( B ) -convergence rates for the exposure-normalized residual density estimator. Simulations for switching Ornstein-Uhlenbeck models illustrate the finite-sample performance of the method.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Estimation of θ is related to parametric inference for discretely observed diffusions, which is well developed; see, for example, Kessler [11] and Gobet [10]. For jump-diffusion models without switching, Shimizu and Yoshida [22], and Ogihara and Yoshida [19] developed related threshold procedures. For models with Markovian switching, statistical inference for ergodic switching diffusions without jumps was developed by Cheng and Masuda [3], who used a Gaussian quasi-likelihood based on the observed regime path. That work is close to the switching component of the present paper, but the absence of jumps eliminates the thresholding problem and the infinite-dimensional nuisance component considered here. Regime-switching Lévy models have also been studied in financial econometrics by Chevallier and Goutte [4], but their setting is not the present high-frequency inference problem for a state-dependent switching jump diffusion with nonparametric regime-wise Lévy densities.

texts.orig: Estimation of s i is related to nonparametric Lévy-measure estimation from high-frequency data. Comte and Genon-Catalot [5] constructed estimators for Lévy processes over a long time interval and obtained L 2 -type risk bounds away from the origin, while Shimizu [21] studied density estimation for discretely observed diffusion processes with jumps. These approaches rely on the small-time approximation of the increment law by the Lévy measure, whose refinements were established by Figueroa-López [6] and Figueroa-López and Houdré [8]. Threshold methods for separating continuous and jump increments go back, in the present statistical context, to Mancini [15] and Shimizu and Yoshida [22]. The additional issue in our model is that the effective observation time is random and regime dependent; consequently, the normalization must be by regime exposure time rather than by the number of detected large increments.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To the best of our knowledge, these two directions have not been combined for discretely observed regimeswitching jump diffusions with unknown continuous coefficients and nonparametric regime-wise Lévy densities. The central idea of this paper is to separate the statistical roles of small and large increments. Small increments are used to estimate the drift and diffusion parameters through a truncated Gaussian quasilikelihood. The truncation removes the leading effect of large jumps, so that the unknown Lévy densities enter the first-stage procedure only as nuisance components. We prove consistency and asymptotic normality of the resulting Gaussian quasi-maximum likelihood estimator (GQMLE).
texts.text: To the best of our knowledge, these two directions have not been combined for discretely observed regimeswitching jump diffusions with unknown continuous coefficients and nonparametric regime-wise Lévy densities. The central idea of this paper is to separate the statistical roles of small and large increments. Small increments are used to estimate the drift and diffusion parameters through a truncated Gaussian quasilikelihood. The truncation removes the leading effect of large jumps, so that the unknown Lévy densities enter the first-stage procedure only as nuisance components. We prove consistency and asymptotic normality of the resulting Gaussian quasi-maximum likelihood estimator (GQMLE).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The large increments are then used to estimate the regime-wise Lévy densities. After the drift has been estimated, we form drift-corrected residuals, keep the residuals that pass a jump threshold, and sort them by the observed regime. The normalization is by the empirical exposure time in each regime, rather than by the number of detected jumps. This point is essential: a Lévy density is an intensity density, not the conditional distribution of a jump size given that a jump has occurred. The random exposure times determine the effective observation horizon in each regime and hence enter the stochastic part of the second-stage error.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The density estimator is studied on compact sets away from the origin. Its L 2 ( B ) -error has the standard bias-variance structure of a kernel estimator, together with remainders caused by discretization, thresholding, and the use of residuals. The second-stage result gives a direct convergence guarantee for the feasible residualbased estimator, with the regime exposure time playing the role of the effective observation horizon.
texts.text: The density estimator is studied on compact sets away from the origin. Its L 2 ( B ) -error has the standard bias-variance structure of a kernel estimator, together with remainders caused by discretization, thresholding, and the use of residuals. The second-stage result gives a direct convergence guarantee for the feasible residualbased estimator, with the regime exposure time playing the role of the effective observation horizon.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The nonparametric component is closest in spirit to the threshold kernel estimator of Shimizu [21], where large increments are used as proxies for unobserved jumps. Our purpose is not to improve the basic kernel rate. Rather, we adapt this idea to a semiparametric regime-switching jump diffusion in which the continuous dynamics are unknown and the effective observation horizon is regime dependent. The residual construction provides feasible jump proxies after the first-stage drift estimation, while the exposure normalization targets the regime-wise Lévy intensity density. The estimation is local in the jump size: on a compact set separated from the origin, only local smoothness of the Lévy density is needed. The framework therefore covers both finite-activity and infinite-activity jump mechanisms, allowing singular behavior near the origin, provided the density is smooth on the region where it is estimated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main technical contributions are therefore twofold. First, we establish the asymptotic theory of the truncated GQMLE in the presence of unknown regime-wise Lévy densities. Second, we derive L 2 ( B ) -rates for an exposure-normalized residual kernel estimator of each regime-wise Lévy density. These results combine threshold-based high-frequency estimation with regime-dependent exposure normalization and preliminary estimation of the continuous dynamics.

Small increments of X are governed by the continuous martingale and drift parts and carry information about the parametric pair θ = ( α, γ ) ; large increments are essentially generated by single jumps and carry information about the regime-wise Lévy densities s ⋆ i , i ∈ S . Accordingly:

texts.orig: The estimator (3.4) is motivated by the ideal situation in which the jump times and jump sizes are directly observed. In that case, the empirical jump measure ∑ 0 <t ≤ T δ ∆ X t is available, and a natural estimator of the Lévy density is obtained by smoothing the observed jump sizes ∆ X t and normalizing by the observation horizon T ; see, for example, [5, 7]. In the present discretely observed jump-diffusion model, however, the actual jump sizes are not observed. The drift-corrected residuals ̂ Y i,j are therefore used as proxies for the unobserved jump sizes.

texts.orig: Table 4 gives Monte Carlo summaries for the GQMLE. In Example 1 the diffusion parameters are estimated very accurately, with RMSEs that decrease monotonically from S1 to S3. The drift errors are larger, as expected from the slower √ T -rate, but their studentized standard deviations stay close to one up to Monte Carlo error. In Example 2 the drift components are less stable, since the tempered-stable jump component produces many small increments near the truncation boundary; nonetheless, increasing T and refining h improves all RMSEs, and the studentized dispersions remain of the correct order.
texts.text: Table 4 gives Monte Carlo summaries for the GQMLE. In Example 1 the diffusion parameters are estimated very accurately, with RMSEs that decrease monotonically from S1 to S3. The drift errors are larger, as expected from the slower √ T -rate, but their studentized standard deviations stay close to one up to Monte Carlo error. In Example 2 the drift components are less stable, since the tempered-stable jump component produces many small increments near the truncation boundary; nonetheless, increasing T and refining h improves all RMSEs, and the studentized dispersions remain of the correct order.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main systematic effect is the positive bias of ˆ γ 1 in Example 2, which decreases from 0 . 0170 (S1) to 0 . 0128 (S3); the bias of ˆ γ 2 is smaller, decreasing from 0 . 0051 to 0 . 0036 . This is a finite-sample truncation effect: in the infinite-activity model, small and moderate jumps below the threshold are absorbed into the continuous martingale variation and thereby inflate the diffusion estimate. The effect is more pronounced in regime 1, whose tempered-stable density is larger near the origin at the chosen parameters, and it weakens as h , u , and η decrease.

The plug-in estimator ˆ s i,n uses the estimated drift ˆ α n in the residual correction, whereas the infeasible oracle estimator ˜ s i,n uses the true α ⋆ . The plug-in and oracle errors are nearly identical in every setting, in line with the theoretical negligibility of the first-stage drift error. The L 2 ( B ) -errors decrease from S1 to S3 in both examples and both regimes. The sup-norm errors decrease less regularly-particularly in Example 2-because they are governed by the largest local discrepancy near the inner boundary of B ; overall they confirm that the nonparametric estimator benefits from the longer horizon and finer mesh.
